food_name: Wu Kok (Deep-Fried Taro Puff with Pork Filling)
region_of_origin: Guangdong, China; widely served in Singapore and Hong Kong yum cha restaurants
food_subgroup: Deep-fried dim sum; taro-shell pastry with savoury pork mince filling, 1 piece approximately 60g

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn does not have a specific entry for wu kok / deep-fried taro puff. Closest parent would be a fried filled pastry or dim sum category, which is not individually enumerated in FoodOn as of 2024.
ontology.foodex2_description: Pastry, fried — closest FoodEx2 parent for deep-fried filled taro pastry; no specific dim sum code exists in FoodEx2 matrix.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: No specific FoodEx2 code for wu kok or Cantonese dim sum fried taro puff. A0FJP (fried pastry) used as closest defensible parent.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept for wu kok or deep-fried taro puff dim sum item.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard piece of wu kok weighs approximately 60g and is typically served as a single portion at a yum cha table.

nutrient_source.title: Wu Kok (deep-fried taro puff) — HPB Singapore Food Composition Database
nutrient_source.organization: Health Promotion Board Singapore — Food Composition Database (Foodo)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Food Composition Database entry for wu kok (deep-fried taro puff with pork filling). Values are reported per 100g edible portion.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of taro and deep-fried taro products — analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database taro entries and peer-reviewed literature on deep-frying effects on starch digestibility
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database; peer-reviewed literature
gi_source.source_match_notes: Steamed taro has a GI of approximately 56 (medium) per University of Sydney GI Database. Deep-frying taro starch gelatinises and disrupts the starch granule structure, significantly elevating GI. Published literature on deep-fried starchy foods supports GI elevation to the high range (70–80). GI of 75 (high) is used as a defensible analogous estimate for deep-fried taro puff. GL per serving = (75 × 38g available carbohydrate per 100g × 60g serving / 100) / 100 = 17.1.

hypertension.guidance: One piece of wu kok contributes approximately 330mg sodium, representing 14–22% of the recommended daily sodium limit (1500–2300mg); patients with hypertension should limit to one piece and avoid combining with other high-sodium dim sum items such as siu mai or cheung fun with soy sauce in the same meal.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High GI (~75), high GL (~17.1 per piece), high energy density (258 kcal per piece), and high fat content (16.2g per piece)
type2_diabetes.guidance: Wu kok is among the least appropriate dim sum choices for patients with Type 2 Diabetes due to its combination of high GI (approximately 75), high glycaemic load (GL ~17.1 per piece), and high caloric density (258 kcal per 60g piece); patients should avoid or strictly limit to one piece per yum cha occasion and prioritise steamed low-GI alternatives such as har gau or steamed rice rolls.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High saturated fat (11g/100g; 6.6g per piece) from lard in shell and deep-frying medium; trans fat 0.1g/100g
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Wu kok contains 6.6g saturated fat per piece, representing approximately 30–44% of the recommended daily saturated fat limit (15–22g), primarily from lard incorporated into the taro shell dough and the deep-frying process; patients with cardiovascular disease should avoid wu kok or limit strictly to one piece on rare occasions, as regular consumption would substantially elevate saturated fat intake and cardiovascular risk.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Very high saturated fat (6.6g per piece) from lard; pro-inflammatory dietary pattern
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: The high saturated fat content from lard in wu kok is relevant to cardiovascular risk management in rheumatoid arthritis patients; limit strictly to 0–1 piece on special occasions and avoid regular consumption.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Wu kok (芋角, yù jiǎo) is a classic Cantonese dim sum item served at yum cha. It consists of a deep-fried shell made from mashed taro mixed with wheat starch and lard, encasing a savoury filling of seasoned pork mince with soy sauce, oyster sauce, and sesame oil. The hallmark of a well-made wu kok is its delicate lacy, honeycomb-patterned crust (鬆化, sōng huà) formed during deep-frying, which gives the exterior a visually striking net-like appearance. The interior is soft and slightly sticky from the taro, contrasting with the crispy outer shell. One standard piece weighs approximately 60g. Nutritionally, wu kok is among the most calorie-dense and fat-rich individual dim sum items: 430 kcal/100g, 27g fat (11g saturated), 38g carbohydrate, and 10g protein per 100g. A single piece provides 258 kcal, 16.2g fat (6.6g saturated), and a glycaemic load of approximately 17.1. The lard content in the shell and the deep-frying process account for the extreme saturated fat and calorie density. Clinically, wu kok is specifically contraindicated or strongly limited for patients with Type 2 Diabetes (high GI ~75, high GL), cardiovascular disease (high saturated fat from lard), and rheumatoid arthritis (pro-inflammatory saturated fat load). A harm-reduction message used in clinical nutrition counselling is: 'Eat only the filling if you cannot resist.' Compared to har gau, one wu kok (60g) contains approximately three times the calories and ten times the fat of one basket of har gau (60g, 4 pieces).

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Food Composition Database (Foodo), an approved primary regional source for Singaporean and Cantonese dim sum dishes. GI is an analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database taro entries and peer-reviewed literature on deep-frying effects on starch digestibility, clearly documented. All mandatory multilingual names are present for this Singaporean-served Cantonese dish.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractionation data for wu kok not available in HPB Singapore Food Composition Database or USDA FoodData Central as of 2026. water_g set to -1: moisture content for wu kok not reported in HPB Singapore Food Composition Database. vitamin_d_mcg is 0.0 (confirmed zero per HPB Singapore source — taro-based fried pastry with pork filling contains negligible vitamin D). GI of 75 is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for wu kok exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. The FoodEx2 code A0FJP (fried pastry) is used as the closest defensible parent; no specific dim sum code exists in the FoodEx2 matrix. Hypertension suitability is marked moderate (not unsuitable) because sodium per piece (330mg) is meaningful but not extreme in isolation; the concern escalates in the context of a full yum cha meal.
